summary: No-ship. The browser fitting is remotely driveable/readable from any webpage on the user's machine, step-id collisions poison the action cache across steps, and the new "ephemeral" browser path leaves profile data behind on disk.

findings.title: Wildcard CORS plus unauthenticated browser endpoints let any visited webpage drive the browser and exfiltrate internal/file-page content
findings.recommendation: Do not expose `/tabs`, `/nav`, `/observe`, `/a11y`, `/screenshot`, `/dom`, `/network`, `/execute`, or the WS upgrades without an internal auth check and strict Origin validation. Remove `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *`, require a capability token on HTTP and WS paths, and restrict navigation to an allowlisted scheme set so an attacker cannot pivot the browser to `file:` or localhost-only targets and read them back.

findings.title: Action-cache keys are only `step.id + fingerprint`, so duplicate step ids let one step poison another step's replayed action
findings.recommendation: Reject duplicate/non-string step ids during automation validation, or key the cache by an unforgeable per-step identity such as step index plus id. Today a duplicated `step.id` on the same page fingerprint will cause later browser steps to reuse the earlier cached action.

findings.title: The default non-persistent launch path is not actually ephemeral because the temp Chromium profile is never deleted
findings.recommendation: Track the temp `user-data-dir` created for non-persistent runs and remove it on shutdown and crash-recovery paths. As written, cookies/session state survive under `/tmp/garrison-browser-*`, which defeats the stated default ephemeral behavior.
